key_insight: A fully differentiable, matrix-free PyTorch phase-field fracture solver can run on CPUs and GPUs without custom compiled extensions while supporting both forward simulation and inverse material identification.
engineering_relevance: It enables scalable fracture simulation and parameter calibration inside modern ML/optimization workflows, making damage mechanics more accessible for large meshes and cross-platform deployment.
method: Element-level finite-element operations are expressed as tensor contractions with scatter-based accumulation, avoiding global sparse stiffness assembly in the explicit dynamic loop.
The solver supports AT1/AT2 regularizations, spectral/volumetric-deviatoric/star-convex energy splits, and plane strain or plane stress options.
A custom backward rule uses implicit differentiation through the CG solve for the bound-constrained damage problem, keeping memory use independent of CG iterations.
evidence_signal: The authors benchmarked four dynamic and two quasi-static fracture cases, reported operation on meshes of about 10^6 nodes on a single NVIDIA A100 GPU, and recovered Gc with relative error below 10^-3 in inverse tests.
watch_item: Performance and accuracy will still depend on the CG tolerance, damage-solve conditioning, and GPU memory bandwidth despite the matrix-free design.
paper_title: A matrix-free, differentiable PyTorch solver for phase-field fracture: Formulation, benchmarks, and inverse analysis
